questions.explanation: Option A overgeneralizes `BECOMES` as a catch-all, ignoring that `EXIT RANGE` and heartbeat exist as distinct, purpose-built operators for exactly these two shapes. Option C's stateless comparison would re-fire on every reading still outside the band (the flapping-alert problem stateful conditions exist to avoid), unlike `EXIT RANGE`'s transition-only firing. Option D denies Activator's documented stateful vocabulary entirely, which handles both requirements natively with no external tool needed.

questions.title: Why a Refresh Schedule Quietly Stopped Running
questions.question: A semantic model's scheduled refresh has silently stopped triggering. No one manually disabled it. The team checks and finds the credentials for the underlying data source expired several days ago. What's the documented behavior that explains the schedule going silent, and what are the other two refresh-failure cause categories worth checking if credentials turn out fine?

choices.A: Refresh schedules never auto-deactivate; the schedule must have been manually disabled by someone without leaving an audit trail
choices.B: Credential failures alone can never cause a schedule to stop — only timeout and memory/capacity issues are documented as triggers for auto-deactivation, regardless of how many consecutive credential failures accumulate
choices.C: A refresh schedule auto-deactivates after a single failure of any kind, immediately, with no consecutive-failure threshold
choices.D: A refresh schedule auto-deactivates after 4 consecutive failures — credentials are one of three failure-cause categories, alongside timeout (default 5h/attempt, 24h total) and memory/capacity limits

questions.shortAnswer: A scheduled refresh automatically deactivates after 4 consecutive failures — expired credentials are a documented, common trigger for that failure streak. The other two refresh-failure cause categories worth checking are timeout (default 5 hours per attempt, 24 hours total including retries) and memory/capacity limits, both of which produce the same "schedule went silent" symptom for a different underlying reason.
questions.explanation: Option A denies the documented auto-deactivation behavior entirely, sending the team looking for a manual culprit that isn't there. Option B wrongly excludes credentials from the failure-cause list, when they're explicitly one of the three named categories. Option C understates the threshold — it's 4 consecutive failures, not a single one, which matters for correctly interpreting how long a problem had to be occurring before the schedule went quiet.

questions.title: A Deployment Rule for the MatchStats Warehouse
questions.question: Finance wants a data source rule configured for the MatchStats Warehouse's connection, expecting the same flexibility they already have for the leaderboard semantic model. What do they find when they try to configure it?

choices.A: The data source rule configures successfully — Warehouse supports the same rule types as semantic model
choices.B: Warehouse supports a data source rule but not a parameter rule, the same narrower support paginated report gets under deployment pipelines
choices.C: No deployment rule of any kind is available for a Warehouse — connection differences for Warehouse need item-level parameterization instead
choices.D: A data source rule is available for Warehouse, but only after first converting it to a mirrored database

questions.shortAnswer: Deployment rules are a short, specific list of item types: Dataflow Gen1, semantic model, and paginated report/mirrored database get a data source rule (with Dataflow Gen1 and semantic model also getting a parameter rule), and notebook gets a default lakehouse rule. Warehouse isn't on that list at all — finance's working data source rule on the semantic model doesn't extend to the Warehouse, which needs item-level parameterization to handle connection differences across stages instead.
questions.explanation: Option A wrongly assumes uniform rule support across item types that support deployment rules at all. Option B correctly describes paginated report's narrower support but misapplies it to Warehouse, which has zero deployment-rule support, not a data-source-rule-only subset. Option D invents a mirrored-database-conversion workaround that has nothing to do with how Warehouse deployment rules actually work — mirrored database gets its own data source rule as a distinct item type, converting a Warehouse into one isn't a supported path to unlock rules.

questions.title: The Leaderboard's Guardrail Failure
questions.question: The leaderboard's Direct Lake on OneLake semantic model refresh started failing outright with a guardrail error instead of just running slowly, after the backing Lakehouse table grew into the hundreds of gigabytes unoptimized. What's the fix, and could the team have expected a graceful DirectQuery fallback instead of an outright failure?

choices.A: `OPTIMIZE`/`VACUUM` the source Lakehouse table; no — Direct Lake on OneLake never falls back to DirectQuery on a guardrail breach, it fails outright, unlike Direct Lake on SQL
choices.B: Manually raise the model's guardrail thresholds in the semantic model settings; yes — a graceful DirectQuery fallback should have occurred automatically
choices.C: `OPTIMIZE`/`VACUUM` the source Lakehouse table; yes — Direct Lake on OneLake always falls back to DirectQuery first, and only fails outright as a last resort
choices.D: Nothing can be done once a guardrail is breached — the table must be rebuilt from scratch in a new Lakehouse

questions.shortAnswer: Both Direct Lake variants trace guardrail problems back to the same root cause — the lakehouse table's file-size health — so `OPTIMIZE`/`VACUUM` on the source table is the fix either way. But the two variants behave completely differently on breach: Direct Lake on OneLake has no DirectQuery fallback at all and fails the refresh outright, while Direct Lake on SQL can fall back to DirectQuery (governed by `DirectLakeBehavior`) and keep succeeding, just more slowly. The leaderboard's outright failure is exactly what's expected for the OneLake variant.
questions.explanation: Option B invents a manual guardrail-threshold override that isn't a documented lever, and wrongly expects a fallback that doesn't apply to this variant. Option C gets the fix right but inverts the fallback behavior — DirectQuery fallback is a Direct Lake on SQL characteristic, not a Direct Lake on OneLake one. Option D drastically overstates the remediation needed; `OPTIMIZE`/`VACUUM` addresses the underlying file-size health without any need to rebuild the table.

questions.title: Who Actually Sees the Unmasked Email
questions.question: The trust & safety Contributor sees unmasked player emails during an investigation with no explicit `UNMASK` grant, while the Viewer on the same workspace stays masked. Both lack an explicit grant. What explains the difference?

choices.A: DDM masking is randomly applied per session regardless of role, so this is expected variance, not a role-driven behavior
choices.B: The Contributor must have an undocumented, hidden `UNMASK` grant applied automatically at workspace creation
choices.C: Admin/Member/Contributor all carry implicit `CONTROL`, which bundles `UNMASK` — only Viewer, without that implicit privilege, stays masked
choices.D: This is a misconfiguration — both roles should see the same masked view without an explicit `UNMASK` grant

questions.shortAnswer: This is the Who-Bypasses-What matrix's core DDM fact: Admin, Member, and Contributor all carry implicit `CONTROL` on the database, which bundles `UNMASK` — so any of the three sees unmasked values with no explicit `UNMASK` grant needed at all. Viewer doesn't carry that implicit privilege, so DDM's masking behavior actually applies to a Viewer, exactly matching what the support engineer experienced.
questions.explanation: Option D treats correct, documented, role-driven behavior as a bug to fix. Option A invents session-randomness that has nothing to do with how DDM actually resolves visibility — it's fully deterministic based on role and grants. Option B invents a hidden grant where none is needed; implicit `CONTROL` alone fully explains the Contributor's unmasked view.

questions.title: The Reconnecting Player's Buffered Match Events
questions.question: A player's console reconnects and resends buffered match events several minutes after their 5-minute tumbling window closed. The streaming job's `.withWatermark()` tolerance has already elapsed for that window by the time the events arrive. What actually happens to those late events, and what's the precise role the watermark played?

choices.A: The watermark automatically reopens the closed window and recomputes its aggregate to include the late events, since Delta tables support append-after-close by design and intent, the same way any append-only table recomputes a previously finalized aggregate
choices.B: `.withWatermark()` has no effect on late data of any kind — its only documented purpose is exactly-once deduplication, entirely unrelated to windowed aggregation timing
choices.C: The watermark rejected the events at the ingestion boundary — they never actually entered the stream and were silently dropped by Eventstream before ever reaching the Spark job
choices.D: The events are still processed and land in the underlying data; they're just excluded from that specific tumbling window's already-finalized aggregation result, since the watermark had already bounded and released that window's state

questions.shortAnswer: A watermark never rejects late data from the underlying stream — it only bounds how long window/dedup *state* is retained before being released. Once `.withWatermark()`'s tolerance has elapsed for a given window, that window's aggregation result is finalized and its state is dropped; the reconnecting player's buffered events still land in the data, they just miss inclusion in that specific window's already-closed result.
questions.explanation: Option C wrongly attributes an ingestion-level rejection to the watermark, which operates on aggregation state timing, not stream admission — Eventstream doesn't drop late events either. Option A invents an automatic window-reopening mechanism that doesn't exist; a finalized window's result doesn't get retroactively recomputed. Option B understates the watermark's actual role — it's specifically the timing mechanism for windowed aggregation (and dedup) state, not something unrelated to it.

questions.title: A Malformed Anti-Cheat Payload Under IsTransactional
questions.question: A malformed anti-cheat detection payload arrives, and the `FlaggedSessions` update policy's transform query fails while processing it. The update policy is configured with `IsTransactional: true`. What happens to that batch's raw ingestion into the source table — does the trust & safety team still get the raw, unprocessed payload to investigate later?

choices.A: The raw payload lands in the source table regardless, since transactional only ever affects the derived `FlaggedSessions` table's write, never the source ingestion itself no matter how the update-policy query is configured or how badly it fails
choices.B: Nothing happens differently than non-transactional mode would — `IsTransactional` only changes logging verbosity, not commit behavior
choices.C: The entire ingestion into the source table is rolled back — with `IsTransactional: true`, a failed update-policy query blocks the raw batch from committing at all, so there's no raw payload to fall back on for that batch
choices.D: Only the malformed record within the batch is rolled back; the rest of the batch's raw payloads still commit to the source table normally

questions.shortAnswer: `IsTransactional: true` is precisely the setting that ties the source table's ingestion to the update-policy query's success — if the transform query fails, the entire ingestion into the source table is rolled back, nothing commits, including the raw data. This is the tradeoff the trust & safety team explicitly chose: correctness (never silently corrupting `FlaggedSessions`) over raw-data preservation on a bad batch, the opposite of a non-transactional policy, which would let the raw payload commit even if the derived write failed.
questions.explanation: Option A describes non-transactional behavior, the exact opposite of what `IsTransactional: true` does. Option B dismisses a real, documented commit-behavior difference as a mere logging setting. Option D invents a per-record rollback granularity that isn't how the transactional setting works — the rollback applies to the whole batch's ingestion into the source table, not a single malformed record within it.
